Writing about the right topic in the right keyword space matters more than almost anything else in this dataset.

Recommendation 2: Publish on weekends whenever possible
The is_weekend PDP showed a clear and consistent positive effect — weekend articles have a predicted log(shares) about 0.19 higher than weekday articles, translating to roughly 20% more shares on average. The EDA confirmed this dramatically: Saturday articles are popular 74.52% of the time versus only around 50% on Tuesday and Wednesday. Far fewer articles are published on weekends, so each one faces less competition and gets more reader attention. If the content is not time-sensitive, holding it for Saturday or Sunday is a smart strategy.

Recommendation 3: Write well-researched articles with many external links
The num_hrefs PDP showed a smooth, consistent, monotone positive relationship — every additional link is associated with more predicted shares, all the way up to 150+ links. Articles with zero links had a predicted log(shares) of about 7.4, while articles with 150+ links reached 7.8. Readers and social media users perceive heavily referenced articles as more credible, thorough, and worth sharing. This does not mean stuffing links randomly — it means doing genuine research and citing sources properly.

Recommendation 4: Focus on Social Media and Tech topics
The channel analysis showed that Social Media articles are popular 75.76% of the time and Tech articles 64.12% of the time, compared to only 38.55% for World news and 41.12% for Entertainment. The data channel features also appeared consistently in the feature importance rankings. If there is flexibility in what topics to cover, Social Media and Tech content gives a dramatically higher baseline probability of popularity before a single word is written.

Recommendation 5: Leverage self-referencing strategically
Both self_reference_min_shares and self_reference_avg_sharess ranked highly in GBM (5.98 and 6.07 relative influence) and XGBoost (0.0434 and 0.0425 gain). These features measure how popular the Mashable articles referenced within the current article were. Linking back to previously popular Mashable content is associated with higher shares — possibly because it signals the article is part of an ongoing popular conversation. When writing a new article, deliberately linking to Mashable's own highest-performing related articles is a worthwhile strategy.

Limitations and Future Improvements:
There are several important limitations to acknowledge. The AUC of 0.7422 and RMSPE of 0.8462, while better than the benchmarks, still leave a lot of variance unexplained — predicting virality is genuinely hard and these models are not perfect. All features in this dataset were measured before publication, but some of the strongest predictors like self_reference and kw_avg_avg require knowledge of the existing content landscape, which may not always be easy to control directly. This dataset also covers articles from 2013-2015 on Mashable specifically — reader behavior and platform algorithms have changed significantly since then, and the findings may not generalize perfectly to today's content environment. Future work could incorporate more recent data, add features like social media engagement timing, or use natural language processing on the actual article text rather than just metadata.

---

# Bonus: Stacked Ensemble (15 bonus points) {#bonus}

**Stacking** (also called **stacked generalization**) is an ensemble technique that combines the predictions of multiple diverse models using a **meta-learner**. The idea is that different models capture different patterns in the data, and a meta-learner can learn the optimal way to combine their predictions — often outperforming any individual model.

### How Stacking Works

1. **Train base models**: You already have three base models — Random Forest, GBM, and XGBoost.
2. **Generate out-of-fold predictions**: To avoid data leakage, base model predictions for the meta-learner must be generated on data the base model **did not** train on. Use k-fold cross-validation:
   - Split training data into k folds
   - For each fold, train each base model on the other k−1 folds and predict on the held-out fold
   - After all folds, you have OOF (out-of-fold) predictions from each base model for every training observation
3. **Train the meta-learner**: Use the OOF predictions as **new features** (one column per base model) and the original target as the response. A simple model works best — linear regression (for regression) or logistic regression (for classification).
4. **Make final predictions**: For the test set, predict with each base model (trained on full training data), then feed those predictions into the meta-learner.

### Why Stacking Works

Each base model makes **different errors** on different observations. If RF overpredicts for certain types of articles while XGBoost underpredicts for those same articles, a meta-learner can learn to weight their predictions optimally. The key requirement is **model diversity** — stacking works best when base models are substantially different (which RF, GBM, and XGBoost are, due to their different learning mechanisms).

### Your Task

Implement stacking for **both** the regression and classification tasks.

```{r stacking-regression, cache=TRUE}
# 1. Set up k-fold CV (e.g., k = 5) on the training data
# Skip if already computed
if(!exists("stack_rmspe")){

set.seed(42)
k       <- 5
folds   <- cut(sample(1:nrow(train_reg)), breaks = k, labels = FALSE)

# Create empty OOF prediction vectors
oof_rf  <- numeric(nrow(train_reg))
oof_gbm <- numeric(nrow(train_reg))
oof_xgb <- numeric(nrow(train_reg))

# 2. For each fold:
#    a. Train RF, GBM, XGBoost on the training folds
#    b. Predict on the held-out fold → store as OOF predictions
for(fold in 1:k){
  val_idx   <- which(folds == fold)
  train_idx <- which(folds != fold)
  fold_train <- train_reg[train_idx, ]
  fold_val   <- train_reg[val_idx, ]
  rf_fold <- randomForest(log_shares ~ .,
                          data  = fold_train,
                          ntree = 500)
  oof_rf[val_idx] <- predict(rf_fold, newdata = fold_val)
  gbm_fold <- gbm(log_shares ~ .,
                  data              = fold_train,
                  distribution      = "gaussian",
                  n.trees           = best_gbm_row$best_n_trees,
                  shrinkage         = best_gbm_row$shrinkage,
                  interaction.depth = best_gbm_row$interaction.depth,
                  n.minobsinnode    = best_gbm_row$n.minobsinnode,
                  bag.fraction      = best_gbm_row$bag.fraction,
                  verbose           = FALSE)
  oof_gbm[val_idx] <- predict(gbm_fold,
                               newdata = fold_val,
                               n.trees = best_gbm_row$best_n_trees)
  fold_matrix     <- model.matrix(log_shares ~ . - 1, data = fold_train)
  fold_val_matrix <- model.matrix(log_shares ~ . - 1, data = fold_val)
  fold_label      <- fold_train$log_shares
  xgb_fold <- xgboost(
    x                = fold_matrix,
    y                = fold_label,
    nrounds          = best_xgb_row$best_nrounds,
    max_depth        = best_xgb_row$max_depth,
    learning_rate    = best_xgb_row$eta,
    subsample        = best_xgb_row$subsample,
    colsample_bytree = best_xgb_row$colsample_bytree,
    objective        = "reg:squarederror",
    eval_metric      = "rmse"
  )
  oof_xgb[val_idx] <- predict(xgb_fold, newdata = fold_val_matrix)
  cat("Fold", fold, "complete\n")
}

# 3. After all folds: you have 3 columns of OOF predictions (one per model)
oof_df <- data.frame(
  rf_pred    = oof_rf,
  gbm_pred   = oof_gbm,
  xgb_pred   = oof_xgb,
  log_shares = train_reg$log_shares
)

# 4. Train a meta-learner: lm(shares ~ rf_pred + gbm_pred + xgb_pred)
meta_reg <- lm(log_shares ~ rf_pred + gbm_pred + xgb_pred, data = oof_df)

# 5. For the test set:
#    a. Predict with each base model (trained on FULL training data)
#    b. Feed into the meta-learner
test_rf_pred  <- predict(rf_reg_model, newdata = test_reg)
test_gbm_pred <- predict(gbm_final,
                         newdata = test_reg,
                         n.trees = best_gbm_row$best_n_trees)
test_xgb_pred <- predict(xgb_final, newdata = test_matrix)
test_stack_df <- data.frame(
  rf_pred  = test_rf_pred,
  gbm_pred = test_gbm_pred,
  xgb_pred = test_xgb_pred
)
stack_pred_reg <- predict(meta_reg, newdata = test_stack_df)

# 6. Compute test RMSPE for the stacked ensemble
stack_rmspe <- sqrt(mean((test_reg$log_shares - stack_pred_reg)^2))

} # end if

# 7. Compare to individual models
cat("\nMeta-learner coefficients:\n")
print(summary(meta_reg)$coefficients)
cat("\nModel Comparison:\n")
cat("Linear Regression RMSPE:", round(lm_rmspe,   4), "\n")
cat("Random Forest RMSPE:    ", round(rf_rmspe,   4), "\n")
cat("GBM RMSPE:              ", round(gbm_rmspe,  4), "\n")
cat("XGBoost RMSPE:          ", round(xgb_rmspe,  4), "\n")
cat("Stacked Ensemble RMSPE: ", round(stack_rmspe, 4), "\n")
```

```{r stacking-classification, cache=TRUE}
# Same approach but:
# - Base model predictions are predicted PROBABILITIES (not class labels)
# - Meta-learner: glm(popular ~ rf_prob + gbm_prob + xgb_prob, family = binomial)
# - Evaluate with AUC

# Skip if already computed
if(!exists("stack_auc")){

set.seed(42)
folds_cls <- cut(sample(1:nrow(train_cls)), breaks = k, labels = FALSE)

# Create empty OOF probability vectors
oof_rf_prob  <- numeric(nrow(train_cls))
oof_gbm_prob <- numeric(nrow(train_cls))
oof_xgb_prob <- numeric(nrow(train_cls))

for(fold in 1:k){
  val_idx   <- which(folds_cls == fold)
  train_idx <- which(folds_cls != fold)
  fold_train_cls <- train_cls[train_idx, ]
  fold_val_cls   <- train_cls[val_idx, ]

  # Random Forest probabilities
  rf_fold_cls <- randomForest(popular ~ .,
                              data  = fold_train_cls,
                              ntree = 500)
  oof_rf_prob[val_idx] <- predict(rf_fold_cls,
                                  newdata = fold_val_cls,
                                  type    = "prob")[, "Popular"]

  # GBM probabilities
  fold_train_gbm <- fold_train_cls %>%
    mutate(popular = ifelse(popular == "Popular", 1, 0))
  fold_val_gbm <- fold_val_cls %>%
    mutate(popular = ifelse(popular == "Popular", 1, 0))

  gbm_fold_cls <- gbm(popular ~ .,
                      data              = fold_train_gbm,
                      distribution      = "bernoulli",
                      n.trees           = best_gbm_cls_row$best_n_trees,
                      shrinkage         = best_gbm_cls_row$shrinkage,
                      interaction.depth = best_gbm_cls_row$interaction.depth,
                      n.minobsinnode    = best_gbm_cls_row$n.minobsinnode,
                      bag.fraction      = best_gbm_cls_row$bag.fraction,
                      verbose           = FALSE)
  oof_gbm_prob[val_idx] <- predict(gbm_fold_cls,
                                    newdata = fold_val_gbm,
                                    n.trees = best_gbm_cls_row$best_n_trees,
                                    type    = "response")

  # XGBoost probabilities
  fold_matrix_cls     <- model.matrix(popular ~ . - 1, data = fold_train_cls)
  fold_val_matrix_cls <- model.matrix(popular ~ . - 1, data = fold_val_cls)
  fold_label_cls      <- ifelse(fold_train_cls$popular == "Popular", 1, 0)

  xgb_fold_cls <- xgboost(
    x                = fold_matrix_cls,
    y                = factor(fold_label_cls),
    nrounds          = best_xgb_cls_row$best_nrounds,
    max_depth        = best_xgb_cls_row$max_depth,
    learning_rate    = best_xgb_cls_row$eta,
    subsample        = best_xgb_cls_row$subsample,
    colsample_bytree = best_xgb_cls_row$colsample_bytree,
    objective        = "binary:logistic",
    eval_metric      = "auc"
  )
  oof_xgb_prob[val_idx] <- predict(xgb_fold_cls,
                                    newdata = fold_val_matrix_cls)

  cat("Fold", fold, "complete\n")
}

# Train meta-learner on OOF probabilities
oof_cls_df <- data.frame(
  rf_prob  = oof_rf_prob,
  gbm_prob = oof_gbm_prob,
  xgb_prob = oof_xgb_prob,
  popular  = train_cls$popular
)

meta_cls <- glm(popular ~ rf_prob + gbm_prob + xgb_prob,
                data   = oof_cls_df,
                family = binomial)

# Predict on test set
test_rf_prob  <- predict(rf_cls_model,
                         newdata = test_cls,
                         type    = "prob")[, "Popular"]
test_gbm_prob <- predict(gbm_cls_final,
                         newdata = test_cls_gbm,
                         n.trees = best_gbm_cls_row$best_n_trees,
                         type    = "response")
test_xgb_prob <- predict(xgb_cls_final, newdata = test_matrix_cls)

test_stack_cls_df <- data.frame(
  rf_prob  = test_rf_prob,
  gbm_prob = test_gbm_prob,
  xgb_prob = test_xgb_prob
)

stack_prob_cls <- predict(meta_cls,
                          newdata = test_stack_cls_df,
                          type    = "response")

# Evaluate with AUC
stack_pred_obj <- prediction(stack_prob_cls, test_cls$popular)
stack_auc_perf <- performance(stack_pred_obj, measure = "auc")
stack_auc      <- round(stack_auc_perf@y.values[[1]], 4)

} # end if

# Print results
cat("\nMeta-learner coefficients:\n")
print(summary(meta_cls)$coefficients)
cat("\nModel Comparison:\n")
cat("Logistic Regression AUC:", round(logit_auc, 4), "\n")
cat("Random Forest AUC:      ", round(rf_auc,    4), "\n")
cat("GBM AUC:                ", round(gbm_auc,   4), "\n")
cat("XGBoost AUC:            ", round(xgb_auc,   4), "\n")
cat("Stacked Ensemble AUC:   ", stack_auc,            "\n")
```

#### **Bonus Question (15 points):** Does the stacked ensemble outperform the individual models? Report the stacked ensemble's RMSPE (regression) and AUC (classification) alongside the individual model results. Examine the meta-learner coefficients — which base model received the most weight? What does this tell you about the relative strengths of the base models? Discuss when stacking is most beneficial and when it might not help.

**Your Answer:**
Does the stacked ensemble outperform the individual models?
For regression, the stacked ensemble achieved an RMSPE of 0.8460, which is marginally better than XGBoost alone at 0.8462 — a difference of just 0.0002. For classification, the stacked ensemble achieved an AUC of 0.7423, compared to XGBoost's 0.7422 — again an extremely tiny improvement of just 0.0001. In both cases the stack technically wins but by such a small margin that it is practically negligible.

Regression meta-learner coefficients — which base model received the most weight:
XGBoost received the highest coefficient of 0.699, meaning the meta-learner assigned roughly 70% of the weight to XGBoost predictions. Random Forest received 0.155 and GBM received 0.163. The meta-learner essentially learned that XGBoost is the most reliable base model and leaned heavily on it, using RF and GBM only as minor corrections.

Classification meta-learner coefficients:
The pattern is even more striking in classification. XGBoost received a coefficient of 4.016, by far the largest and highly significant with a p-value of 1.19e-21. Random Forest received 0.950 and is also significant with a p-value of 8.17e-06. GBM however received a negative coefficient of -0.082 and is completely insignificant with a p-value of 0.833 — meaning the meta-learner effectively ignored GBM entirely in the classification task. This tells us that for classification, XGBoost and Random Forest are capturing genuinely useful and complementary signals, while GBM adds nothing beyond what the other two already provide.

What does this tell us about the relative strengths of the base models:
Across both regression and classification, XGBoost is clearly the dominant model. The meta-learner independently discovered the same ranking I found in individual model comparisons without being told. The fact that GBM was ignored in classification but contributed in regression suggests GBM's strength is more in continuous prediction tasks than binary classification on this particular dataset.

When is stacking most beneficial and when might it not help:
Stacking works best when base models make very different errors on different observations. In this dataset, RF, GBM, and XGBoost are all tree-based methods that tend to make similar errors on the same observations, which is why the improvement from stacking is so tiny — just 0.0002 in RMSPE and 0.0001 in AUC. Stacking would be much more powerful if I combined fundamentally different model types such as a neural network, a linear model, and a tree-based model together. The key lesson is that model diversity drives stacking performance, and three variations of tree-based models simply do not provide enough diversity to generate meaningful gains beyond what the best individual model already achieves.
---

# Submission Checklist

Before submitting, ensure:

- [ ] All code chunks run without errors
- [ ] All questions answered with explanations (not just code output)
- [ ] Plots are properly labeled with titles and axis labels
- [ ] **Part 1:** Summary statistics table, popularity distribution, channel/weekday tables, ggplot visualizations
- [ ] **Part 2:** All 4 regression models trained (LM, RF, GBM, XGBoost), comparison table with RMSPE, mean RMSPE ± SD for best model
- [ ] **Part 3:** All 4 classification models trained (Logistic, RF, GBM, XGBoost), comparison table with AUC, mean AUC ± SD for best model, confusion matrix
- [ ] **Part 4:** Feature importance plots, PDPs, actionable insights
- [ ] Tables formatted with `kable`/`kableExtra` (not raw console output)
- [ ] No use of `caret` or `h2o` for hyperparameter tuning
- [ ] Team members listed in author field
- [ ] LLM usage disclosed (if applicable)
- [ ] Both `.qmd` and `.html` files submitted

---

**Final Note:** This project would be a great addition to your portfolio. Make sure to include it in your resume and LinkedIn profile. You can also use it as a case study in interviews. Good luck!
